heading: MODULUS QUARTUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the fourth Modulus with Concentus I, "Structura," on who built the heavenly city. Zorzi draws on Apocalypse 21 (John's vision of the New Jerusalem) and Matthew 25 to argue that God as supreme Architect (Calotechnius, "good builder") prepared a triple dwelling for humanity: the celestial kingdom, the present world as pilgrimage, and the Church built on Christ and the twelve apostolic foundations. The three edifices are consimilar — each made by the same Artificer and bearing his image — so knowledge of one allows inference about the others. Ezekiel's and John's measurements are invoked as calibrating standards for this triple architecture, and the liturgical hymn Urbs beata Hierusalem is cited alongside Scripture to affirm the city's construction from living stones.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 108 opens the fourth *Modulus* of *De Harmonia Mundi* with its first *Concentus*, entitled "Structura — Quis eam struxerit" (Structure — Who built it). The passage establishes the cosmological and ecclesiological framework for the entire *Modulus*: a vision of three analogically related dwellings (*hospitia*), all designed by a single divine Architect, whose proportional harmony allows the contemplative intellect to ascend from the lowest to the highest.

## The Vision of the New Jerusalem (Apocalypse 21)

Zorzi opens by citing John's Apocalypse as the clearest declaration of the city's builder:

> Vidi civitatem sanctam Ierusalem descendentem de coelo, a Deo paratam, sicut sponsam ornatam viro suo.

<cite>p. 2518 (fol. XCVIv)</cite>

The descent of the heavenly Jerusalem is simultaneously the descent of her *Calotechnius* — a Greek term Zorzi glosses in Latin as "bonus Architectus, opifex, et ornator" (good builder, craftsman, and adorner). This figure descends precisely in order to receive us into the city and return us to heaven. The word *Calotechnius* (from Greek *kalos* + *techne*) is unusual and signals Zorzi's Neoplatonic-humanist register: God as the beautiful craftsman, an echo of Plato's Demiurge in the *Timaeus*.

## The Liturgical Gloss: Urbs beata Hierusalem

Zorzi immediately reinforces the Apocalyptic citation with the ancient Office hymn *Urbs beata Hierusalem*, quoting it directly:

> Urbs beata Ierusalem dicta pacis visio, quae construitur in coelis vivis ex lapidibus, et angelis coronata, ut sponsata comite.

<cite>p. 2518 (fol. XCVIv)</cite>

The hymn's "living stones" (*vivis lapidibus*) ties the celestial architecture back to the Petrine and Pauline metaphor of the faithful as *lapides vivi* (1 Peter 2:5). The celestial city is made of angelic and human souls, not inert matter — a living, harmonic structure.

## The Triple Hospitium

The theological core of the passage is Zorzi's doctrine of the *triplex hospitium* — the three dwellings prepared by the *summus Architectus* for the human guest (*humanus hospes*):

1. **The celestial kingdom** (*regnum coeleste*) and the mansions of the blessed — the eternal destination, prepared in the mind of the craftsman from before the foundation of the world.
2. **The present world** (*mundanum hospitium*) — the place of pilgrimage, which, precisely because it is "elegantissime disposito" (most elegantly arranged), gives us a foretaste of the eternal dwelling.
3. **The Church** — built upon Christ as foundation and upon the twelve apostolic foundations, constructed from living stones and measured by the measurements (*mensurae*) of Ezekiel and John.

The scriptural warrant for the first hospitium is Matthew 25:34:

> Venite benedicti Patris mei, possidete paratum vobis regnum a constitutione mundi.

<cite>p. 2518 (fol. 428r)</cite>

The phrase "a constitutione mundi" (from the foundation of the world) is significant: it anchors the celestial city in the *mens opificis* (the mind of the craftsman) as an eternal idea pre-existing creation — a Platonic Form that the temporal world and the Church each imperfectly instantiate.

## The Doctrine of Analogical Architecture

The most philosophically important claim in this chunk is Zorzi's assertion of *consonantia* among the three hospitia:

> Et tanta est horum trium consonantia, ut ex uno aliud coniicere possimus, dummodo bene intelligamus cuiuslibet aedificii naturam, et dispositionem, quamvis hoc, quod nunc habitamus, feculentum, et infimum, et illud ad quod tendimus expurgatum sit et exquisitissimum.

<cite>p. 2518-2519</cite>

This is the methodological key to the entire *Modulus*: because all three edifices are made by the same Artificer (*ab eodem Artifice constructae*) and each bears his image (*ipsiusque imaginem gerit unaquaeque pro captu suo*), they are symbolically and analogically related. Reading the proportions of one dwelling allows inference about the others. The present world, though "feculentum et infimum" (turbid and lowest), is an image of the purified celestial one.

## Hierarchical Mediation and the Church

Zorzi introduces a crucial hierarchical point: the middle dwelling — the Church — is explicitly described as spiritual (*spirituale*), and it is the necessary passage point for anyone seeking to ascend to the celestial:

> id etiam quod medium est, et cuius auspiciis ad illud supernum properamus spirituale sit, domus videlicet, et civitas ecclesiae in cuius sinu vel ambitu, ut contineamur, necesse est, si coeleste ac supernum conscendere volumus.

<cite>p. 2519</cite>

This passage encapsulates Zorzi's ecclesiology: the Church is not merely an institution but a cosmic mediating structure, the spiritual city whose proportions mirror both the world below and the heavenly Jerusalem above. To be contained (*contineamur*) within its embrace is the necessary condition for ascent.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

The triple hospitium doctrine synthesizes several threads running throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*: (1) the Neoplatonic principle that the many images of a single Form are analogically readable against each other; (2) the Kabbalistic notion of correspondences between cosmic levels; (3) the architectural numerology derived from Ezekiel 40-48 and Apocalypse 21, which Zorzi treats as literal blueprints for the harmonic structure of reality. The *Calotechnius* figure bridges the Platonic Demiurge and the Christian Creator, while the *consonantia* of the three dwellings recapitulates the overarching harmonic thesis: all of creation is a single musical composition whose parts resonate with one another across ontological levels.
